description: Ivory-white marble mausoleum built by Mughal emperor Shah Jahan in memory of his wife Mumtaz Mahal. Considered the finest example of Mughal architecture, combining Persian, Islamic and Indian styles.
34 monasteries and temples hewn out of the rock face of the Charanandri Hills, representing Buddhist, Hindu and Jain rock-cut architecture from the 6th to 11th century CE.
Rock-cut Buddhist cave monuments with paintings and sculptures from 2nd century BCE to 480 CE, considered masterpieces of Buddhist religious art.
13th-century CE Sun Temple at Konark, dedicated to the sun god Surya, conceived as a gigantic chariot with elaborately carved stone wheels, pillars and walls.
Group of Hindu and Jain temples built by the Chandela dynasty between 950 and 1050 CE, famous for their Nagara-style architectural symbolism and erotic sculptures.
Collection of rock-art cave temples dedicated mainly to the Hindu god Shiva, located on Elephanta Island in Mumbai Harbour, dating from the 5th to 8th centuries CE.
Ruins of the 14th century Vijayanagara Empire capital spread across 4000 hectares, featuring temples, palaces, elephant stables, market streets and a sacred centre.
Group of sanctuaries and monuments built during the Pallava dynasty from the 7th and 8th centuries on the Coromandel Coast, featuring rathas, mandapas and the Shore Temple.
Abandoned Mughal imperial capital built by Emperor Akbar in the late 16th century, featuring mosques, palaces and public buildings in a blend of Persian and Indian architectural styles.
Walled Mughal imperial city and seat of government of the Mughal Empire under successive emperors from Akbar to Aurangzeb, containing palaces, audience halls, mosques, and gardens.
Intricately constructed stepwell on the banks of the Saraswati River, built as a memorial to a king in the 11th century, featuring seven levels of stairs with sculpted panels.
Series of fortifications built across northern China spanning thousands of kilometres, constructed over centuries by multiple Chinese states and empires for protection against nomadic invasions.
Imperial palace complex in central Beijing that served as the home of emperors and their households for almost 500 years, housing 980 surviving buildings with 8728 rooms.
Temple complex near Siem Reap, the largest religious monument in the world at 162.6 hectares, originally constructed as a Hindu temple for the Khmer Empire, gradually transforming to Buddhism.
9th century Mahayana Buddhist temple and the world's largest Buddhist temple, comprising 9 stacked platforms (6 square and 3 circular) topped by a central dome and decorated with 2672 relief panels.
Archaeological zone on the plains of central Myanmar containing the remains of thousands of Buddhist temples, pagodas, monasteries and other religious structures built during the Pagan Kingdom.
Ancient citadel above Athens containing the remains of several architecturally significant buildings of classical antiquity, most notably the Parthenon, Erechtheion, and the Temple of Athena Nike.

Elliptical amphitheatre in the centre of Rome, the largest ancient amphitheatre ever built, capable of holding between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators, used for gladiatorial contests and public spectacles.
Ancient Roman city near modern Naples, destroyed and buried under 4–6 metres of volcanic ash following the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 CE, providing an exceptional snapshot of Roman life.

Prehistoric monument on Salisbury Plain consisting of a ring of standing stones, each around 4 metres high, 2 metres wide and weighing around 25 tonnes, aligned with the sunrise on the summer solstice.
Archaeological city famous for its rock-cut architecture and water conduit system, established possibly as early as the 4th century BCE as the capital of the Nabataean Kingdom.
Ancient necropolis containing the Great Pyramid (Khufu), Pyramid of Khafre, Pyramid of Menkaure and the Great Sphinx, built during the Old Kingdom period around 2560–2504 BCE.
Two massive rock-cut temples originally carved out of the mountainside during the reign of Pharaoh Ramesses II in the 13th century BCE, relocated in 1968 due to the flooding of Lake Nasser.
Ancient Thebes with its Necropolis, including the temples of Karnak and Luxor and the necropoleis of the Valley of the Kings and Valley of the Queens.
15th-century Inca citadel situated on a mountain ridge 2,430 metres above sea level, featuring dry-stone walls and elaborate astronomical alignments, abandoned a century after its construction.
Pre-Columbian city built by the Maya civilization in the Terminal Classic period, featuring the step-pyramid El Castillo, dedicated to the feathered serpent deity Kukulcan.
Ancient Mesoamerican city northeast of Mexico City, featuring the Pyramid of the Sun and Pyramid of the Moon, representing one of the largest cities in the pre-Columbian Americas.

Isolated volcanic island with 900 monumental stone statues (moai) created by the Rapa Nui people between the 13th and 16th centuries, representing the most outstanding example of a human society that flourished in total isolation.

Hub of ancestral Puebloan culture between 850 and 1150 CE featuring the most impressive pre-Columbian remains north of Mexico, with massive multi-storied stone buildings and roads linking outlier communities.
Former royal residence and gardens located southwest of Paris, originally a hunting lodge for Louis XIII, transformed by Louis XIV into one of the largest palaces in the world with 2,300 rooms.
Island commune in Normandy featuring a tidal island and the surrounding bay, with a medieval Benedictine abbey that became one of Europe's major pilgrimage destinations.
Palatine city built by the Nasrid dynasty Muslim rulers of the Emirate of Granada in medieval Andalusia, featuring palaces, courts, towers, gardens and fountains in intricate Islamic architecture.

Works of Antoni Gaudí in and around Barcelona, including the still-unfinished Sagrada Família basilica, Casa Batlló, Casa Milà, Park Güell, and Casa Vicens, representing an outstanding creative contribution.
Nazi German concentration and extermination camp complex during World War II, site of the largest mass murder in a single location in human history, inscribed as a symbol of the Holocaust.
Historic fortified complex in the heart of Moscow containing the official residence of the President of Russia, several cathedrals, the State Armory and the Grand Kremlin Palace.
Outstanding examples of Baroque and neoclassical architecture in St Petersburg and surrounding areas, including the Hermitage Museum, Peter and Paul Fortress, and Catherine Palace.

Ruins of the capital of the Kingdom of Zimbabwe, built between the 11th and 15th centuries CE from granite blocks without mortar, representing the largest pre-colonial structure in sub-Saharan Africa.
City on the edge of the Sahara Desert that was an intellectual and spiritual capital of Islam in the 15th and 16th centuries, containing three great mosques and 16 cemeteries and mausoleums.
Island in Table Bay where political prisoners including Nelson Mandela were held during the apartheid era, now a symbol of the triumph of the human spirit over adversity.
Eleven medieval monolithic cave churches carved from solid red volcanic rock in the 12th and 13th centuries by King Lalibela, forming a Jerusalem in Africa for Ethiopian Christians.
Outstanding natural park featuring the annual migration of 1.5 million wildebeest and 250,000 zebra, with rich predator populations including lion, leopard, cheetah and wild dog.
Vast crater formed from a collapsed volcanic caldera containing one of Africa's most dense concentrations of wildlife, and the Laetoli hominid footprints dated to 3.6 million years ago.

Volcanic archipelago in the Pacific Ocean that inspired Charles Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection, with unique flora and fauna including giant tortoises, marine iguanas and blue-footed boobies.

site_name: Amazon Rainforest - Central Amazon Conservation Complex
description: Largest protected area in the Amazon basin, encompassing one of the world's highest concentrations of biodiversity with exceptional aquatic habitats including rivers, lakes, flooded forests and islands.
First national park in the world, featuring half of the world's geysers, abundant wildlife including bison herds, grizzly bears and wolves, and a complete display of the geothermal processes.
World's largest coral reef system composed of over 2,900 individual reefs and 900 islands stretching for over 2,300 kilometres, visible from space and home to extraordinary diversity of marine life.

Two sacred sites of extraordinary geological and ethnological interest: the monolith Uluru (Ayers Rock) and domed rock formations of Kata Tjuta (the Olgas), of profound spiritual significance to the Anangu people.
Bombed Industrial Promotion Hall, the only structure to remain standing in the area where the first atomic bomb was dropped on 6 August 1945, preserved as a memorial to victims and a symbol of peace.
Series of 83 wooden buildings with sophisticated defensive systems from the 14th to 17th centuries, the finest surviving example of early 17th-century Japanese castle architecture.

Seventeen sites within Kyoto, Uji and Otsu cities encompassing Buddhist temples, Shinto shrines, a castle and a garden, representing outstanding examples of Japanese architectural and garden design over eleven centuries.
Eight places of historic significance representing the ancient capital of Japan from 710–784 CE, including the great wooden structure of Todaiji housing a 15-metre bronze Buddha statue.

site_name: Jeju Volcanic Island and Lava Tubes
description: Three sites of outstanding volcanic features: Geomunoreum lava tube system, the largest and most impressive lava tube caves in the world, Seongsan Ilchulbong tuff cone and Hallasan shield volcano.

Buddhist temple complex in South Gyeongsang Province housing the Tripitaka Koreana, 80,000 wood printing blocks of the complete canon of Buddhist teachings, stored since 1398 in extraordinary conditions.

Ceremonial capital of the Achaemenid Empire, constructed from 518 BCE, with monumental staircases, throne halls and reception rooms, a treasury and living quarters, destroyed by Alexander the Great in 330 BCE.
Capital of the Achaemenid Empire under Cyrus the Great in the 6th century BCE, containing the royal ensemble of Pasargadae, the tomb of Cyrus, and the earliest example of Persian garden design.

Four zones of Istanbul containing the world-famous Archaeological Park with Topkapi Palace, the Hagia Sophia and Sultanahmet Mosque, together representing the successive capitals of the Byzantine and Ottoman Empires.

Archaeological site in south-eastern Turkey dating to 10th-8th millennium BCE, the oldest known megalithic structure, consisting of at least 20 round structures with carved T-shaped limestone pillars.
Ruins of a caravan city in central Syria connecting the Roman Empire with the East, featuring a 2nd-century colonnade, temples of Bel and Baalshamin, theatre, agora, and funerary towers.

Sacred to three monotheistic religions — Judaism, Christianity and Islam — the Old City contains the Temple Mount with Al-Aqsa Mosque and Dome of the Rock, the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, and the Western Wall.

One of the oldest cities in Central Asia founded in the 7th century BCE, a crossroads and melting pot of the world's cultures featuring the Registan, Bibi-Khanym Mosque, Shah-i-Zinda necropolis and Gur-e-Amir mausoleum.
